Motion To Compel Arbitration And To Dismissstay Judicial Proceedings Request For Stay Pending Hearing
Off Calendar May-24-2023 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT OZUMO SF LLC'S Motion To Compel Arbitration And To Dismiss/Stay Judicial Proceedings Request For Stay Pending Hearing.

Defendant OZUMO SF LLC's motion to compel arbitration and stay is granted.

"The party seeking arbitration bears the burden of proving the existence of an arbitration agreement, and the party opposing arbitration bears the burden on any defense." (Pinnacle Museum Tower Assn. v. Pinnacle Market Development (US), LLC. (2012) 55 Cal.4th 223, 236; see also Rosenthal v. Great Western Fin. Securities Corp. (1996) 14 Cal.4th 394, 413; Gamboa v. Northeast Community Clinic (2021) 72 Cal.App.5th 158, 165 [explaining three-step process to determine existence of arbitration agreement].)

Defendant demonstrates the existence of an arbitration agreement with the plaintiffs.

Plaintiff Zheng
While defendant fails to provide a signed copy of the arbitration agreement with plaintiff Zheng, defendant presents sufficient evidence that plaintiff Zheng did in fact execute the agreement. (Kawaguchi Decl., pars. 9-10.)

Plaintiff Nguyen
Plaintiff Nguyen signed the agreement to arbitrate in March of 2019 as part of the employee onboarding process. (Kawaguchi Decl., pars. 7, 10, Ex. A.) Nguyen's printed signature is sufficient to be binding. (In re Walker (1895) 110 Cal. 387, 393 [any mark can be sufficient for a signature].)

Plaintiff Kim
Plaintiff Kim agreed to arbitrate her claims in 2014. (Umland Decl., pars. 2-5, Ex. A.)

Plaintiff Kim argues that her agreement to arbitrate is unconscionable. The agreement contains a clear and unmistakable delegation clause. (Kawaguchi Decl., Ex. B, par. 7.) The agreement also incorporates the JAMS Rules and JAMS Rule 11 provides that the arbitrator determines arbitrability. (RJN, Ex. A; Rodriguez v. Am. Techs., Inc. (2006) 136 Cal.App.4th 1110, 1123 [parties may incorporate arbitration rules by reference]; Aanderud v. Sup. Ct. (2017) 13 Cal.App.5th 880, 893 [same]; Brinkley v. Monterey Fin. Servs., Inc. (2015) 242 Cal.App.4th 314, 353-54 [same].)

Where there exists a delegation clause, a party raising the defense of unconscionability must show that the delegation clause itself is unconscionable. (Nielsen Contracting, Inc. v. Applied Underwriters, Inc. (2018) 22 Cal.App.5th 1096, 1107-1108.) To the extent plaintiff Kim contends that the delegation clause is unconscionable, that argument fails.

Plaintiff must demonstrate procedural and substantive unconscionability. (Armendariz v. Foundation Health Psychcare Services, Inc. (2000) 24 Cal.4th 83, 114.) Plaintiff argues that the agreement is procedurally unconscionable because it is adhesive. But contracts of adhesion are fully enforceable. (Graham v. Scissor-Tail, Inc. (1981) 28 Cal.3d 807, 819-820.) Plaintiff claims that the agreement is substantively unconscionable because it requires arbitration in Los Angeles. While that provision does not "shock the conscience," it is burdensome and the court severs it. (Armendariz, 24 Cal.4th at 121-122 [court may sever unconscionable contractual provisions].)

Order is signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr., Clerk: M. Goodman, Not Reported. (302/RBU)
